text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic right inguinal hernia as a tap. Clinically, this was a very symptomatic hernia. It's a very standard operation.

The surgeon raises a flap and begins medial dissection in the area of the internal ring, where an unusual veil crosses. The hernia is located on the medial aspect, lateral to the epigastric vessels, resembling a direct hernia within the indirect space.
The surgeon identifies the pubic bone and begins lateral dissection in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully hugs the peritoneum, maintaining the proper plane while avoiding excessive lateral movement. The cord structures are swept off the peritoneum, revealing a veil-like structure anteriorly, visible just above the scissors.

The surgeon identifies the spermatic cord entering the canal lateral to a veil, while the hernia is positioned medial to this veil and lateral to the epigastric vessels. The hernia is reduced, resembling the reduction process of a direct hernia.

The surgeon has reduced the hernia, revealing the space lateral to the epigastric vessels. The indirect hernia is visible, bisected by an interesting veil, with the spermatic cord structures positioned laterally while the hernia was located medially.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the spermatic cord, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon inspects the area for a cord lipoma, identifying nodal tissue that had protruded into the ring and has now been reduced.
The surgeon places a 16 by 12 cm anatomic ProGrip mesh over the hernia site and proceeds to close the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon suspends a large hernia sac and evacuates gas laparoscopically using an NG catheter, ensuring the mesh is properly positioned within the surgical field.
